segments.title: Coreopsis and the Q-ship connection
segments.summary: The opening separates HMS Coreopsis from a similarly named drifter before tracing the Q-ship's decoy service and deployments.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins with a naming warning, then describes the related Q-ship as a concealed-armament decoy vessel. Its service ranged from the Grand Fleet and Mediterranean to Gibraltar, where it operated under false names. The comparison gives context for why this small-ship lineage matters beyond a single hull's name.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes the names and describes Q-ship, Grand Fleet, Mediterranean, and Gibraltar service.

segments.title: A compact hull with useful carrying margin
segments.summary: Coreopsis's loaded tonnage, displacement, dimensions, and draft show how a small sloop still carried a meaningful margin of stores.
segments.body: The video contrasts fully laden tonnage with displacement, highlighting roughly 450 tons available for fuel, stores, armament, and other loads. It then sets out the 262-foot hull, 35-foot beam, and loaded draft. Those figures explain the practical scale of a vessel expected to do more than one job.

evidence.note: The transcript gives loaded and displacement tonnage, carrying difference, length, beam, and draft.

segments.title: Armament and endurance for a heavily armed small ship
segments.summary: Sixteen knots, coal endurance, four-inch and lighter guns, depth charges, and a crew of 92 reveal a small vessel built for demanding service.
segments.body: Coreopsis combined modest speed with a substantial coal load, while carrying quick-firing four-inch guns, 12-pounders, depth charges, and sometimes smaller guns. Dr. Clarke calls these heavily armed little ships and notes a complement of 92. The inventory shows why the type could undertake armed patrol, escort, or specialist work despite its size.

evidence.note: The transcript lists speed, coal, gun and depth-charge armament, and the complement.

segments.title: Why sloops and minesweepers overlapped
segments.summary: Coreopsis illustrates how early twentieth-century sloops and minesweepers shared a multi-purpose design logic that made them fundable in peacetime.
segments.body: Although the type could serve as a Q-ship, Dr. Clarke stresses that it was first ordered for minesweeping. He treats the sloop and minesweeper as overlapping solutions in the 1910s through 1930s: a small navy ship needed several credible roles. That flexibility mattered politically, because peacetime construction required a clearer justification than wartime emergency building.

evidence.note: The transcript links the type to minesweeping, Q-ship work, multi-purpose small ships, and peacetime procurement.
